I need concrete details about the implementation, not just high-level descriptions. Look at official Anthropic docs, GitHub discussions, and any blog posts explaining the system.

content.text:
I now have everything I need for a complete, authoritative analysis. Let me compile the full findings.

---

## Analysis: Claude Code Skills and Memory Systems

### Summary

Claude Code's skills system is a filesystem-based capability extension mechanism built on the open [Agent Skills](https://agentskills.io) standard, with Anthropic-specific extensions. A skill is a directory containing a `SKILL.md` entry point; the old `.claude/commands/*.md` pattern has been merged into skills and still works. The memory system is a separate two-layer architecture: CLAUDE.md files (human-written, always loaded) and auto memory (Claude-written markdown files at `~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/`, capped at 200 lines or 25KB on load). This repo uses both — four project skills in `.claude/skills/`, each with a `SKILL.md` + `command/` subdirectory, plus an auto-memory directory at `~/.claude/projects/-Users-jeevanpillay-Code--lightfastai-lightfast/memory/`.

---

### Key Findings

#### Finding 1: Skills — File Structure

- **Confidence**: High (verified against official docs + local filesystem)
- **Claim**: A skill is a directory named after the slash command it creates. The only required file is `SKILL.md`. Additional files (docs, templates, scripts) are optional and referenced from `SKILL.md`. A legacy `command/*.md` file still works identically, but skills are the preferred form.
- **Quote**: "A file at `.claude/commands/deploy.md` and a skill at `.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md` both create `/deploy` and work the same way. Your existing `.claude/commands/` files keep working." — https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills
- **Provenance**: Official Claude Code docs (code.claude.com), confirmed against local files in this repo.

Canonical directory structure:
```
my-skill/
├── SKILL.md           # Required entry point
├── reference.md       # Optional supporting file
├── examples/
└── scripts/
    └── helper.py
```

#### Finding 2: Skills — Discovery and Loading at Runtime

- **Confidence**: High (official docs, confirmed against behavior described in GitHub issues)
- **Claim**: Skill descriptions are always loaded into context so Claude knows what is available. However, full skill body content only loads when a skill is invoked (either by you or by Claude). Two frontmatter flags control this behavior: `disable-model-invocation: true` removes the description from context entirely (Claude never auto-triggers it); `user-invocable: false` keeps it in Claude's context but hides it from the `/` menu.
- **Quote**: "In a regular session, skill descriptions are loaded into context so Claude knows what's available, but full skill content only loads when invoked." — https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills
- **Provenance**: Official docs. Corroborated by the invocation table in the frontmatter reference section.

The three states:

| Frontmatter | You can invoke | Claude auto-invokes | Description in context |
|---|---|---|---|
| (default) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| `disable-model-invocation: true` | Yes | No | No |
| `user-invocable: false` | No | Yes | Yes |

Skill discovery is also path-aware: Claude Code watches skill directories for live file changes and automatically discovers `.claude/skills/` in nested subdirectories (relevant for monorepos).

#### Finding 3: Skills — Frontmatter Schema (SKILL.md)

- **Confidence**: High (copied verbatim from official docs at https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills#frontmatter-reference)
- **Claim**: All frontmatter fields are optional. Only `description` is strongly recommended. The full field set is:

| Field | Required | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `name` | No | Uses directory name if omitted. Lowercase, numbers, hyphens only, max 64 chars. |
| `description` | Recommended | Claude uses this to decide when to auto-invoke. Truncated at 1,536 chars in listing. |
| `when_to_use` | No | Appended to `description` in the skill listing, counts toward the 1,536-char cap. |
| `argument-hint` | No | Shown in autocomplete. E.g. `[issue-number]`. |
| `disable-model-invocation` | No | `true` = manual-only. Default: `false`. |
| `user-invocable` | No | `false` = hide from `/` menu. Default: `true`. |
| `allowed-tools` | No | Space-separated or YAML list. Grants permission without per-use approval. |
| `model` | No | Override model for this skill. |
| `effort` | No | `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh`, `max`. Overrides session effort level. |
| `context` | No | `fork` = run in isolated subagent context. |
| `agent` | No | Subagent type when `context: fork`. Options: `Explore`, `Plan`, `general-purpose`, or custom. |
| `hooks` | No | Skill-lifecycle hooks. |
| `paths` | No | Glob patterns. Claude only auto-activates when working with matching files. |
| `shell` | No | `bash` (default) or `powershell`. |

- **Provenance**: Official docs. A GitHub issue (#25380) confirms the VS Code extension's validator incorrectly rejected these Anthropic-extended fields, treating them as non-standard against the base Agent Skills open standard — confirming these fields are real Claude Code extensions on top of the open standard.

#### Finding 4: How Skill Matching and Invocation Work

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: There is no keyword-pattern trigger system embedded in frontmatter. Claude performs semantic judgment based on the `description` and `when_to_use` fields to decide whether to auto-invoke a skill. You can also type `/skill-name` directly. The `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in skill content receives anything typed after the slash command name.
- **Quote**: "Claude uses skills when relevant, or you can invoke one directly with `/skill-name`." — https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills
- **Provenance**: Official docs. Note: a community blog (claudecodeguides.com) claims there is a "TRIGGER" frontmatter block and a multi-step auto-invocation pipeline with semantic scoring — this is NOT documented in official sources and is contradicted by the official frontmatter schema. Treat that source as speculative or describing community conventions in skill bodies, not a system feature.
- **Additional mechanics**:
  - Once invoked, skill content enters the conversation as a single message and stays for the session.
  - After `/compact`, Claude re-attaches the most recent invocation of each skill up to a combined 25,000-token budget (first 5,000 tokens per skill), starting from most recently invoked.
  - Shell commands via `` !`cmd` `` syntax run at skill load time (preprocessing), not by Claude.

#### Finding 5: The `command/` Subfolder — Local Pattern in This Repo

- **Confidence**: High (direct file observation)
- **Claim**: This repo's four project skills (lightfast-sdk, lightfast-inngest, lightfast-db, lightfast-changelog) each use a two-file pattern not described in the official docs: a `SKILL.md` with reference material and a `command/<skill-name>.md` file that acts as a thin slash-command wrapper. The command files contain `skill({ name: '<skill-name>' })` calls in their workflow steps and `$ARGUMENTS` pass-through. This is a project-specific architectural pattern, not a built-in feature called "command/". The `command/*.md` files here are discoverable as skills because `.claude/skills/<name>/command/` is a subdirectory of the skill — but based on the official docs, only `SKILL.md` is the entry point; the `command/` files would not auto-load unless explicitly referenced from `SKILL.md`.
- **Provenance**: Direct read of local files at `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/.claude/skills/`.

The `command/lightfast-sdk.md` file has its own frontmatter `description` field and calls `skill({ name: 'lightfast-sdk' })` internally, suggesting it acts as an invocable slash command that loads the parent skill's reference material. This is a delegation pattern using the `skill()` function call in the markdown body — which is distinct from the (confirmed non-functional) `skill:` frontmatter field discussed in GitHub issue #27570.

#### Finding 6: Memory System — MEMORY.md Index and Auto Memory

- **Confidence**: High (official docs + leaked source analysis from multiple independent engineers)
- **Claim**: Auto memory stores files at `~/.claude/projects/<project-hash>/memory/`. The project hash is derived from the git repository root, so all worktrees share one memory directory. The entry point `MEMORY.md` is an index; the first 200 lines or 25KB (whichever comes first) are loaded into every session. Topic files (e.g. `debugging.md`) are NOT loaded at startup — Claude reads them on demand.
- **Quote**: "The first 200 lines of `MEMORY.md`, or the first 25KB, whichever comes first, are loaded at the start of every conversation. Content beyond that threshold is not loaded at session start." — https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory
- **Provenance**: Official Anthropic docs. Independently corroborated by two separate leaked-source analyses (milvus.io, mem0.ai) which read the actual TypeScript source from the accidentally shipped source map in version 2.1.88.

Memory directory structure:
```
~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/
├── MEMORY.md          # Index, max 200 lines / 25KB loaded per session
├── debugging.md       # Topic file, loaded on demand
├── api-conventions.md
└── ...
```

#### Finding 7: Memory System — How Retrieval Works (vs. Common Misconception)

- **Confidence**: High (leaked source corroborated by two independent analyses)
- **Claim**: Claude does NOT use embeddings or vector search to find relevant memory files. It makes a separate Sonnet API call that scans the `MEMORY.md` index (filenames and one-line descriptions), then picks up to 5 files deemed relevant. This is a language-model judgment call on filenames/descriptions, not semantic vector search.
- **Quote**: "The process: scan all memory files, extract their filenames and descriptions, send that manifest to Sonnet, ask it to pick the most relevant ones. Max 5 files returned." — https://milvus.io/blog/claude-code-memory-memsearch.md (leaked source analysis)
- **Provenance**: Leaked TypeScript source (version 2.1.88, `src/memdir/`). Two independent engineers arrived at identical conclusions. Official docs do not describe this mechanism at this level of detail.

#### Finding 8: Memory System — Auto Dream and KAIROS

- **Confidence**: Medium (leaked source, not officially documented)
- **Claim**: The source contains an "Auto Dream" consolidation process that runs in the background when more than 24 hours have passed since last cleanup AND at least 5 new sessions have accumulated. It replaces vague time references with dates, resolves contradictions, and keeps `MEMORY.md` under 200 lines. A separate unreleased "KAIROS" mode (compile-time feature flag, not in any public build) would make Claude Code run as an always-on background daemon. Neither is described in official docs.
- **Quote**: "You can also type 'dream' to run it manually. The process runs in a background sub-agent — like actual sleep, it won't interrupt your active work." — https://milvus.io/blog/claude-code-memory-memsearch.md
- **Provenance**: Leaked source map from version 2.1.88. Not in official docs. Treat as implementation detail subject to change.

#### Finding 9: The `/memory` Command

- **Confidence**: High (official docs)
- **Claim**: `/memory` lists all CLAUDE.md, CLAUDE.local.md, and rules files loaded in the current session, provides a toggle for auto memory on/off, and provides a link to open the auto memory folder. Selecting any file opens it in your editor. Auto memory can also be controlled via `autoMemoryEnabled` in project settings or `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_AUTO_MEMORY=1` environment variable.
- **Quote**: "The `/memory` command lists all CLAUDE.md, CLAUDE.local.md, and rules files loaded in your current session, lets you toggle auto memory on or off, and provides a link to open the auto memory folder." — https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory
- **Provenance**: Official Anthropic docs.

#### Finding 10: Four Memory Types (Enforced by Source Code)

- **Confidence**: Medium (leaked source, not in official docs)
- **Claim**: The source constrains memory to four explicit categories: `user` (role and preferences — private only), `feedback` (corrections, validated approaches), `project` (architectural decisions, deadlines), and `reference` (pointers to external systems). The code explicitly states: if information is derivable from the current codebase via grep or git, it should NOT be saved as a memory.
- **Provenance**: Leaked source `src/memdir/`, reported by both milvus.io and mem0.ai analyses independently.

---

### Source Chain

1. https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills — Official Claude Code documentation (authority: official docs, current)
2. https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory — Official Anthropic memory documentation (authority: official docs, current)
3. https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/equipping-agents-for-the-real-world-with-agent-skills — Anthropic engineering blog (authority: maintainer communication, October 2025)
4. https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/25380 — GitHub issue confirming extended frontmatter fields (authority: maintainer/community, February 2026)
5. https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/27570 — GitHub issue confirming `skill:` delegation field does NOT exist and is silently ignored (authority: community + test evidence, February 2026)
6. https://milvus.io/blog/claude-code-memory-memsearch.md — Leaked source analysis of `src/memdir/` TypeScript (authority: community, derived from actual source, April 2026)
7. https://mem0.ai/blog/how-memory-works-in-claude-code — Independent leaked source analysis confirming memory architecture (authority: community, derived from actual source, April 2026)

---

### Conflicts

- The community blog at claudecodeguides.com describes a "TRIGGER" frontmatter block and a multi-stage semantic scoring pipeline for auto-invocation. The official docs at code.claude.com describe no such field — the full frontmatter schema does not include `TRIGGER`. The mechanism is simply the `description` field being read by Claude. The community blog appears to be describing patterns in skill body text as if they were system features.

- The VS Code extension validator (as of version 2.1.39, issue #25380) only accepts base Agent Skills standard fields (`compatibility`, `description`, `license`, `metadata`, `name`) and rejects Claude Code extended fields like `allowed-tools`, `context`, `agent`, `hooks`. The official docs document all of these as valid. The official docs are authoritative here — the validator was bugged, not the docs.

---

### Gaps

- The exact mechanism by which Claude decides to auto-invoke a skill (the actual model call, its prompt, how descriptions are scored) is not described in official docs. The leaked source gives partial insight but that code is from version 2.1.88 and may have changed.
- The `skill()` function call used in this repo's `command/*.md` files (e.g. `skill({ name: 'lightfast-sdk' })`) does not appear in any official documentation. Whether this is a real invocation mechanism or just instructional text for Claude to parse is unclear from documentation alone.
- The `when_to_use` frontmatter field appears in the official docs table but without a separate detailed explanation of how it interacts with `description` during auto-invocation scoring beyond "appended to description in the skill listing."
- Auto Dream trigger conditions (24h + 5 sessions) and KAIROS come exclusively from the leaked source and have no official documentation. Their behavior may have changed in subsequent versions.
- Official docs do not document the four memory type categories (`user`, `feedback`, `project`, `reference`) at all — this comes only from the leaked source analysis.

---

### Local Relevance

This repo is at `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/.claude/skills/` and contains four project-scoped skills:

- `lightfast-sdk` — MCP tools for searching across GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, Apollo
- `lightfast-inngest` — (not read in detail)
- `lightfast-db` — Postgres read-only query skill via `postgres` MCP server
- `lightfast-changelog` — (not read in detail)

Each skill uses a two-file pattern: `SKILL.md` (reference material + frontmatter `name` and `description`) plus `command/<skill-name>.md` (slash-command wrapper with its own `description` frontmatter and a `skill({ name: '...' })` call). This is a local architectural convention, not a documented Claude Code feature. The `command/` subdirectory is not a special location Claude Code recognizes — only `SKILL.md` is the official entry point. The `command/*.md` files would be treated as additional supporting files in the skill directory, and they would need to be explicitly referenced from `SKILL.md` or directly invokable via their own frontmatter if placed in `.claude/commands/` instead.

The auto-memory for this project lives at `/Users/jeevanpillay/.claude/projects/-Users-jeevanpillay-Code--lightfastai-lightfast/memory/`, confirmed by the MEMORY.md referenced in the system context loaded into this conversation.

content.text:
Here's what I found. Claude Code's approach is actually very close to what you want:

## How Claude Code Does It

**Skills loading:**
- Skill **descriptions** (from frontmatter) are always in context — the agent always knows what's available
- Full skill **body** only loads when invoked (by the model or user)
- The model decides when to invoke based on the `description` field — no keyword matching, pure LLM judgment

**Memory:**
- `MEMORY.md` is an index (max 200 lines), always loaded
- Individual topic files are loaded on-demand (model picks up to 5 relevant ones per session)
- Four types: `user`, `feedback`, `project`, `reference`
- A background "dream" process consolidates memories periodically

**The `command/` pattern in this repo** is actually a local convention you built — not a Claude Code built-in. The official structure is just `SKILL.md` as entry point. Your `command/*.md` files act as thin invocation wrappers that call `skill({ name: '...' })` to load the reference material.

---

## Proposed `.lightfast` Design

```
.lightfast/
├── SPEC.md                              # Company context (always loaded)
├── skills/
│   ├── notify-critical-error/
│   │   ├── SKILL.md                     # Reference: what this skill does, when, how
│   │   └── command/notify-critical-error.md  # Execution template
│   ├── suggest-publish/
│   │   ├── SKILL.md
│   │   └── command/suggest-publish.md
│   └── track-active-work/
│       ├── SKILL.md
│       └── command/track-active-work.md
└── memory/
    ├── MEMORY.md                        # Index (always loaded)
    ├── active-work.md                   # "User is building X"
    ├── recent-incidents.md              # "Prod had Y issue on 2026-04-15"
    └── team-patterns.md                 # "Deploys happen Tue/Thu"
```

**Runtime flow (on every `platform/event.stored`):**

```
1. Load system prompt (internal)
2. Load SPEC.md (org context)
3. Load all skill descriptions (frontmatter only — cheap)
4. Load MEMORY.md index + pick relevant memory files
5. Inject event payload
6. Agent decides:
   - Invoke skill(s)? → load full SKILL.md + command, execute with tools
   - Update memory? → write to memory/
   - Do nothing? → skip
```

**Key insight:** The memory folder is how it "knows what the user is building." The `track-active-work` skill's job is specifically to maintain `memory/active-work.md` — watching commits, PRs, branch activity and updating a running summary. Other skills read that context when deciding whether to act.

---

## The command/ folder question

Adopting Claude's two-file pattern makes sense for your case too:

- **SKILL.md** = reference material (when to fire, what context matters, what tools to use)
- **command/*.md** = execution template (step-by-step instructions the agent follows when it decides to invoke the skill)

The separation is useful because the agent reads SKILL.md descriptions to decide *whether* to act, and only loads the full command template *when* it acts. Keeps token cost low for the 90% of events where the agent does nothing.

---

**Open questions:**

1. **Memory persistence** — stored in the repo (`.lightfast/memory/`) or in your platform DB? Repo means it's versioned but requires git commits. DB means it's live but invisible to the user.

2. **Memory consolidation** — Claude Code's "dream" runs after 24h + 5 sessions. Your equivalent would be... periodic? After N events? A separate scheduled Inngest function?

3. **Skill authoring** — do users write these by hand, or do you want an onboarding flow that scaffolds common skills (notify on error, track deployments, etc.)?

content.text:
**1. Git commits via sandbox** — yeah that's clean. You already have the GitHub connection, the agent writes to `.lightfast/memory/`, commits with a message like `chore(lightfast): update active-work memory`, pushes. Full audit trail, user can see exactly what the agent "thinks" by reading the repo. Simple.

**2. "Dream"** — bad name from Claude Code's internals. It's just memory compaction. After a while you accumulate 50 memory files that overlap or contradict each other. "Dream" is a background process that:
- Merges redundant memories
- Removes stale ones (project that shipped 3 months ago)
- Resolves contradictions
- Keeps the index under 200 lines

For you this would just be a periodic Inngest cron — maybe weekly — where the agent re-reads all its memories and consolidates. Not critical for v1 though. You'll know you need it when `MEMORY.md` gets noisy.

**3. User writes skills themselves** — got it. No scaffolding.

---

So the v1 scope is:

1. Define the `.lightfast/` schema (SPEC.md + skills/ + memory/)
2. New Inngest function on `platform/event.stored` that runs the agent
3. Agent loads context (SPEC.md + skill descriptions + memory index + event)
4. Agent decides to act (invoke skill + use tools) or update memory or skip
5. If memory changes, commit to repo via GitHub API

Perfect! Now I have all the information needed. Let me compile a comprehensive report:

## Comprehensive Lightfast Agent Runtime Overview

I've thoroughly explored the Lightfast codebase. Here's what exists:

---

## 1. ANSWER AGENT & SUPPORTING PIECES

### Answer Route Handler
**File:** `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/apps/app/src/app/(api)/v1/answer/[...v]/route.ts`

Implements POST (new stream) and GET (resume stream) handlers. Key patterns:
- Extracts `agentId` and `sessionId` from URL path segments
- Creates dual auth context (Clerk org + userId + authType + apiKeyId)
- Builds system prompt with org context and cached repo index
- Initializes agent with `createAgent()` + `fetchRequestHandler()`

### createAgent Function
**File:** `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/core/ai-sdk/src/core/primitives/agent.ts`

Factory function returns `Agent<TRuntimeContext, TTools>` instance:
```typescript
export function createAgent<TRuntimeContext, TTools extends ToolFactorySet>
  (options: AgentOptions<TRuntimeContext, TTools>): Agent<TRuntimeContext, TTools>
```

**Key Interfaces:**
- `LightfastConfig<TRuntimeContext, TTools>`: system prompt, tools, cache, createRuntimeContext()
- `VercelAIConfig<TOOLS>`: model, toolChoice, stopWhen, onChunk, onFinish, onStepFinish, experimental_transform
- `AgentOptions` = LightfastConfig + VercelAIConfig merged
- `Agent.buildStreamParams()`: resolves tool factories, applies caching, returns streamText params

### fetchRequestHandler
**File:** `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/core/ai-sdk/src/core/server/adapters/fetch.ts`

Type signature:
```typescript
export async function fetchRequestHandler<
  TAgent extends Agent<any, any>,
  TRequestContext extends RequestContext,
  TFetchContext,
>(options: FetchRequestHandlerOptions<TAgent, TRequestContext, TFetchContext>): Promise<Response>
```

**Responsibilities:**
- Validates HTTP method (POST for new stream, GET for resume)
- Extracts messages from request body
- Creates system + request context
- Calls `streamChat()` or `resumeStream()` from runtime
- Handles errors and returns formatted HTTP responses

### AnswerRedisMemory Class
**File:** `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/apps/app/src/ai/runtime/memory.ts`

Implements `Memory<UIMessage, AnswerMemoryContext>` interface:
- Uses Upstash Redis (KV_REST_API_URL + KV_REST_API_TOKEN)
- **Ephemeral storage:** 1-hour TTL on all data (transient workspace search conversations)
- **Key patterns:** `answer:session:{sessionId}:*`, `answer:stream:{streamId}`
- **Methods:**
  - `appendMessage()`: JSON array append to Redis
  - `getMessages()`: fetch message history
  - `createSession()`: store session metadata
  - `createStream()` + `getActiveStream()`: manage resumable streams
  - `clearActiveStream()`: cleanup after stream completion

### buildAnswerSystemPrompt Function
**File:** `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/apps/app/src/ai/prompts/system-prompt.ts`

```typescript
export function buildAnswerSystemPrompt(options: AnswerPromptOptions): string
```

**Parameters:**
- `modelId`: for token budgeting (defaults to Claude Sonnet 4.5)
- `org.name`: organization name
- `org.repoIndex`: cached repository content from indexed repo

**Implementation:**
- Uses `@repo/prompt-engine` for composable prompt building
- Builds context via `buildPromptContext()` with:
  - `activeTools: ["orgSearch"]`
  - `features: { temporalContext, style, toolGuidance, userContext }`
  - `temporalContext.currentTimestamp`: ISO datetime
  - `userContext.org`: name, repoIndex, repos, integrations
- Returns built prompt string via `buildPrompt(context, ANSWER_PROVIDERS)`

### orgSearchTool
**File:** `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/packages/app-ai/src/org-search.ts`

Tool factory function:
```typescript
export function orgSearchTool(): ToolFactory<LightfastAnswerRuntimeContext>
```

**Definition:**
- Returns `createTool<LightfastAnswerRuntimeContext>({...})`
- **Input:** `SearchRequestSchema` (validated search query)
- **Output:** `SearchResponseSchema` (ranked results with scores, snippets, source types, metadata)
- **Execution:** delegates to `context.tools?.orgSearch?.handler(input)`
- **Runtime injection:** Handler configured in route's `createRuntimeContext()` → calls `searchLogic(authContext, input, randomUUID())`

**Type:** `LightfastAnswerRuntimeContext` includes:
- `clerkOrgId`, `userId`, `authToken`
- `tools.orgSearch.handler`: async search function

---

## 2. PLATFORM EVENT SCHEMA & POST-PROCESSING

### Event Schema Definition
**File:** `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/api/platform/src/inngest/schemas/platform.ts`

**platform/event.stored** event schema:
```typescript
"platform/event.stored": z.object({
  clerkOrgId: z.string(),
  eventExternalId: z.string(),
  sourceType: z.string(),
  significanceScore: z.number(),
  correlationId: z.string().optional(),
})
```

### Post-Processing: Event Storage Workflow
**File:** `/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/api/platform/src/inngest/functions/platform-event-store.ts`

**Function ID:** `platform/event.store` (triggered by `platform/event.capture` events)

**8-Step Workflow:**
1. **Generate IDs**: nanoid for externalId, timestamp for replay safety
2. **Check duplicate**: query by sourceId (idempotency gate)
3. **Check allowed**: validates event type against provider config + integration active status
4. **Evaluate significance**: `scoreSignificance(sourceEvent)` (metadata annotation, not a gate)
5. **Extract entities**: structural categories from relations (commits, branches, PRs, issues, deployments)
6. **Store event row**: insert into `orgEvents` table with title, content, metadata, sourceId, significance score
7. **Emit downstream events:**

   - **platform/event.stored** → triggers notification dispatch
8. **Complete job**: success output with observationId, observationType, entitiesExtracted count

- `platform-entity-graph.ts`: builds entity relationship graph

content.text:
Good. Here's the full picture. The accretive steps:

---

## Step-by-step build path

### Phase 1: Schema & Config Parsing
**Ship: the `.lightfast/` contract**

1. Define the `.lightfast/` directory schema — SPEC.md, skills/, memory/ structure
2. Build a config loader in `api/platform/src/lib/` that reads `.lightfast/` from a GitHub repo via `get-file-contents` API (you already have this endpoint)
3. Parse skill frontmatter (descriptions) and SPEC.md content into a typed structure

**What you have:** GitHub `get-file-contents` API already works. You just need a loader that walks `.lightfast/skills/*/SKILL.md` and extracts frontmatter.

**Missing:** GitHub write/commit API endpoint — you'll need to add `PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}` to the GitHub provider for memory commits.

---

### Phase 2: Agent Runtime (Inngest function)
**Ship: the agent runs on every event**

4. New Inngest function: `platform/skill.run` triggered by `platform/event.stored`
5. It loads the org's `.lightfast/` config (SPEC.md + skill descriptions + MEMORY.md)
6. Builds a system prompt: internal instructions + SPEC.md + skill descriptions + memory index
7. Runs a single agent turn with the event payload as the "user message"
8. Agent output is either: invoke a skill, update memory, or `{ action: "skip" }`

**Model:** Use the same `gateway("anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6")` pattern you already use. Sonnet is fast/cheap enough for triage on every event.

**Tools available to the agent:**
- `orgSearch` (already built)
- `invokeSkill` — loads full SKILL.md + command, re-runs agent with skill instructions
- `writeMemory` — writes a file to `.lightfast/memory/` and commits
- `sendNotification` — Slack/Discord webhook (new, simple)

---

### Phase 3: Skill Execution
**Ship: skills actually do things**

9. When agent invokes a skill, load the full `SKILL.md` + `command/*.md`
10. Re-run the agent with skill content injected as instructions + event as context
11. Skill execution has access to the full tool set (search, notify, GitHub comment, etc.)

---

### Phase 4: Memory Loop
**Ship: agent remembers across events**

12. Add `PUT` to GitHub provider (create/update file + commit)
13. `writeMemory` tool: writes to `.lightfast/memory/`, updates `MEMORY.md` index, commits
14. Memory is loaded on every run, so the agent accumulates context over time

---

### Phase 5: Polish
15. Token budget management (truncate skills/memory if too large)
16. Deduplication (don't re-fire on replayed events)
17. Observability (log every agent decision in `orgIngestLogs` or similar)

---

## What to build first

**Phase 1 + 2 together** — get the Inngest function firing on every event, loading SPEC.md, and making a single LLM call that returns "skip" for most events. That proves the loop works. Then layer in skills and memory.

Want me to start on the config loader and Inngest function?

file.content:
/**
 * Zod schemas for lightfast.yml validation
 *
 * @see docs/architecture/phase1/dx-configuration.md
 */

import { z } from "zod";

/**
 * Zod schema for lightfast.yml configuration
 *
 * @example
 * ```yaml
 * version: 1
 * store: docs-site
 * include:
 *   - apps/docs/src/content/docs/**\/*.mdx
 *   - apps/docs/src/content/api/**\/*.mdx
 * ```
 */
export const LightfastConfigSchema = z.object({
  /**
   * Configuration version (currently only version 1 is supported)
   */
  version: z.literal(1),

  /**
   * Human-readable store name (unique per organization)
   * Used as the store key in the system
   *
   * Constraints:
   * - Max 20 characters
   * - Only lowercase alphanumeric and hyphens
   * - No leading/trailing hyphens
   * - No consecutive hyphens
   *
   * @example "docs-site" or "api-reference"
   */
  store: z
    .string()
    .min(1, "Store name must not be empty")
    .max(20, "Store name must be 20 characters or less")
    .regex(
      /^[a-z0-9-]+$/,
      "Store name must be lowercase alphanumeric with hyphens only"
    )
    .refine(
      (name) => !/^-|-$|--/.test(name),
      "Store name cannot have leading/trailing/consecutive hyphens"
    ),

  /**
   * Array of glob patterns (repo-relative) for files to include in ingestion
   * Must contain at least one pattern
   * @example ["apps/docs/src/content/**\/*.mdx"]
   */
  include: z
    .array(z.string().min(1, "Glob pattern must not be empty"))
    .min(1, "At least one include pattern is required"),
});

/**
 * TypeScript type inferred from LightfastConfigSchema
 */
export type LightfastConfig = z.infer<typeof LightfastConfigSchema>;

content.text:
Got it. So you already have `@repo/app-config` which parses `lightfast.yml` — a v1 config that's just `{ version, store, include }` for content ingestion. The `.lightfast/` directory with skills/memory is the next evolution of this.

Here's the accretive path, given what exists:

---

## Accretive Steps

### Step 1: `.lightfast/` directory schema
Evolve `@repo/app-config` to also recognize a `.lightfast/` folder structure alongside the existing `lightfast.yml`. Define the contract:

```
.lightfast/
├── SPEC.md              # always loaded (replaces/extends lightfast.yml context)
├── skills/
│   └── <skill-name>/
│       ├── SKILL.md     # reference + frontmatter (description)
│       └── command/
│           └── <skill-name>.md   # execution template
└── memory/
    ├── MEMORY.md        # index (always loaded)
    └── *.md             # topic files (loaded on demand)
```

New Zod schema + loader that reads this from GitHub via `get-file-contents`.

### Step 2: GitHub tree reader
You have `get-file-contents` for single files. You need a `getTree` call to list `.lightfast/skills/*/SKILL.md` without knowing names upfront. Add GitHub Trees API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/trees/{sha}?recursive=1`) to the provider.

### Step 3: Inngest function — `platform/agent.run`
New function triggered by `platform/event.stored`. Steps:
1. Load org's `.lightfast/` tree (cached per org, invalidated on push webhook)
2. Build system prompt: internal + SPEC.md + skill descriptions + MEMORY.md
3. Single LLM call with event payload as user message
4. Parse response: `skip` | `invoke:<skill-name>` | `memory:write`

### Step 4: Skill execution
When agent says `invoke:<skill-name>`:
1. Load full SKILL.md + command/*.md for that skill
2. Re-run agent with skill instructions + event + memory context
3. Agent has access to tools: `orgSearch`, `sendNotification` (Slack/Discord webhook), `writeMemory`

### Step 5: Memory writes via GitHub commit
Add `PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}` to GitHub provider. The `writeMemory` tool commits to `.lightfast/memory/` with a machine-authored commit message.

### Step 6: Cache `.lightfast/` config
Cache the parsed tree in Redis (or `orgRepoIndexes`) so you're not hitting GitHub on every event. Invalidate on `push` webhook to the default branch.

---

**Start with Steps 1+2** — get the config schema and tree reader working. Then Step 3 is just wiring it into an Inngest function. Want me to start on the schema evolution in `@repo/app-config`?

input.prompt:
I need to understand exactly HOW Claude Code executes skills at runtime. Not just the file structure, but the actual execution mechanics:

1. When a skill is invoked (either by user typing /skill-name or by Claude auto-invoking), what happens?
   - Is the SKILL.md content injected as a system message? As a user message?
   - Does the command/*.md become the actual prompt?
   - Is it appended to the conversation or does it replace something?

2. The $ARGUMENTS placeholder - how does it work? Is it string-interpolated before injection?

3. The `skill({ name: '...' })` call pattern - is this a real function call or just instructional text that Claude reads and follows?

4. Does Claude Code use a "tool" to invoke skills, or is it purely prompt injection?

5. After a skill loads, does Claude get a fresh turn to respond, or does it continue the same turn?

6. How does the `context: fork` frontmatter work? Does it spawn a subagent?

7. The `allowed-tools` frontmatter - does it actually grant tool permissions or just inform Claude which tools to use?

Search for:
- Claude Code skill execution internals
- How Claude Code loads skills into context
- Claude Code Skill tool implementation
- The actual Skill tool that appears in Claude Code's tool list
- Any leaked source code analysis of skill loading/execution
- GitHub issues discussing skill execution behavior

I want the mechanical truth of how skills execute, not marketing descriptions.

content.text:
I now have everything I need — the actual source code from the leak plus multiple GitHub issues confirming observed runtime behavior. This is comprehensive enough to give a definitive mechanical picture.

---

## Analysis: Claude Code Skill Execution Internals

### Summary

Skills in Claude Code are implemented as a real registered tool called `SkillTool` (internally `SKILL_TOOL_NAME`). When Claude auto-invokes a skill, it makes an actual tool call that Claude Code intercepts and resolves by injecting the rendered `SKILL.md` content as a `isMeta: true` user message into the conversation. When a user types `/skill-name` directly, the CLI bypasses the Skill tool entirely and injects the same user message via a pre-query processing path — which is why PreToolUse hooks do not fire on user-initiated slash commands but do fire on Claude-initiated ones. The `$ARGUMENTS` substitution is string interpolation performed before the content enters the conversation. The `context: fork` frontmatter spawns a genuine isolated sub-agent via `runAgent()`.

---

### Key Findings

#### 1. The Skill Tool is a Real Tool in Claude's Tool List

- **Confidence**: High — verified from leaked `SkillTool.ts` source code
- **Claim**: `SkillTool` is a proper tool object with input schema `{ skill: string, args?: string }`, registered in Claude's tool list alongside `BashTool`, `ReadTool`, etc. Claude can call it the same way it calls any other tool, with a `tool_use` block in its response.
- **Quote**: `export const SkillTool: Tool<InputSchema, Output, Progress> = buildTool({ name: SKILL_TOOL_NAME, searchHint: 'invoke a slash-command skill', ... })` — `src/tools/SkillTool/SkillTool.ts` (leaked source, alex000kim/claude-code mirror)
- **Provenance**: Leaked `SkillTool.ts` via Bun source map bug in npm package v2.1.88, March 31 2026

#### 2. Inline Skill Content Enters the Conversation as a Meta User Message

- **Confidence**: High — verified from leaked source
- **Claim**: For default (non-fork) skills, the `call()` method runs `processPromptSlashCommand()` which produces processed messages. These are tagged with `sourceToolUseID` and returned as `newMessages` on the `ToolResult`. They become `isMeta: true` user messages injected into the conversation history — not a system message, not a new assistant turn, but a specially-flagged user-role message. The official docs confirm this framing: "the rendered SKILL.md content enters the conversation as a single message and stays there for the rest of the session."
- **Quote**: For remote skills: `return { data: ..., newMessages: tagMessagesWithToolUseID([createUserMessage({ content: finalContent, isMeta: true })], toolUseID) }` — `SkillTool.ts` call() / `executeRemoteSkill()`
- **Provenance**: Leaked source + official docs at `code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md`

#### 3. $ARGUMENTS is Plain String Interpolation Before Injection

- **Confidence**: High — confirmed in source and official docs
- **Claim**: `$ARGUMENTS`, `$ARGUMENTS[N]`, `$N`, `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}`, and `${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}` are all string-substituted during `processPromptSlashCommand()` (or the equivalent rendering step) before the content is packaged as a message. The model never sees the placeholder tokens — it only receives the already-interpolated text. Shell-style quoting applies to indexed arguments.
- **Quote**: `finalContent = finalContent.replace(/\$\{CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR\}/g, normalizedDir); finalContent = finalContent.replace(/\$\{CLAUDE_SESSION_ID\}/g, getSessionId())` — `SkillTool.ts`, `executeRemoteSkill()` function
- **Provenance**: Leaked source + official docs string substitution table

#### 4. User-Typed `/skill-name` and Claude-Called `Skill(skill="name")` Are Two Different Code Paths

- **Confidence**: High — proven by GitHub bug reports with reproducible test cases
- **Claim**: When a user types `/skill-name` directly, the CLI processes it in the pre-query path (via `isSlashCommand()` detection in `messageQueueManager.js` and `processSlashCommand` before the API call), injecting the skill content as a user message before the query loop starts. The Skill tool is NOT invoked. When Claude auto-invokes, it issues a real `tool_use` block, which Claude Code handles via `SkillTool.call()`. This is why `PreToolUse: Skill` hooks fire on Claude-initiated calls but NOT on user-initiated `/skill-name` inputs. `UserPromptSubmit` is the only hook that fires for user-typed slash commands.
- **Quote**: "When a user types a slash command (e.g. `/commit`) directly, the Skill tool invocation does not trigger `PreToolUse` or `PostToolUse` hooks... When Claude programmatically invokes the same skill via the Skill tool, `PreToolUse:Skill` and `PostToolUse:Skill` fire as expected." — GitHub issue #24858 (confirmed reproduced)
- **Provenance**: Issues #24858, #42250 (open as of 2026-04-09), cross-referenced with `query.ts` source showing `isSlashCommand()` branch

#### 5. Claude Gets a Fresh Turn After Skill Content Is Injected

- **Confidence**: High — confirmed by official docs and source architecture
- **Claim**: The skill content arrives as a new user message in the conversation. Claude Code then makes a new API call with that expanded message history, giving Claude a fresh response turn to act on the instructions. It does not continue the same turn. The official docs state content "stays there for the rest of the session" — it is permanently part of the conversation history from that point forward, not a one-shot injection.
- **Quote**: "When you or Claude invoke a skill, the rendered SKILL.md content enters the conversation as a single message and stays there for the rest of the session. Claude Code does not re-read the skill file on later turns." — `code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md`
- **Provenance**: Official docs + consistent with tool result → new API call pattern visible in `query.ts`

#### 6. `context: fork` Spawns a Real Isolated Sub-Agent via `runAgent()`

- **Confidence**: High — verified from leaked source
- **Claim**: When a skill has `context: fork`, `SkillTool.call()` routes to `executeForkedSkill()`, which calls `prepareForkedCommandContext()` and then `runAgent()`. The sub-agent has its own isolated message history — it does NOT have access to the parent conversation. The skill content becomes the sub-agent's prompt. When the sub-agent completes, `extractResultText()` extracts a summary, which is returned as the Skill tool result to the parent conversation. This result is a `tool_result` block, not a user message.
- **Quote**: `const { modifiedGetAppState, baseAgent, promptMessages, skillContent } = await prepareForkedCommandContext(command, args || '', context); ... for await (const message of runAgent({ agentDefinition, promptMessages, ... })) { agentMessages.push(message) }` — `SkillTool.ts`, `executeForkedSkill()` function
- **Quote**: The `mapToolResultToToolResultBlockParam()` for forked skills returns `content: 'Skill "${result.commandName}" completed (forked execution).\n\nResult:\n${result.result}'` — same file
- **Provenance**: Leaked `SkillTool.ts`

#### 7. `allowed-tools` Grants Real Runtime Permissions, Not Just Hints

- **Confidence**: High — verified from source
- **Claim**: The `allowed-tools` frontmatter values are extracted from the processed command and stored in `allowedTools`. The `contextModifier` function in `SkillTool.call()` mutates `toolPermissionContext.alwaysAllowRules.command` to add those tools. This is a real permission grant active for the rest of the session after skill invocation, not advisory text. It does not restrict — all tools remain callable, and deny rules in settings still apply.
- **Quote**: `modifiedContext = { ...modifiedContext, getAppState() { const appState = previousGetAppState(); return { ...appState, toolPermissionContext: { ...appState.toolPermissionContext, alwaysAllowRules: { ...appState.toolPermissionContext.alwaysAllowRules, command: [...new Set([...existing, ...allowedTools])] } } } } }` — `SkillTool.ts`, `call()` contextModifier
- **Provenance**: Leaked source + official docs: "grants permission for the listed tools while the skill is active, so Claude can use them without prompting you for approval. It does not restrict which tools are available."

#### 8. Discovery vs. Full Load: Lazy Loading Is the Architecture

- **Confidence**: High — confirmed in official docs and GitHub issues
- **Claim**: At startup, only skill descriptions (frontmatter: name + description + when_to_use) are loaded into context as a skill listing — approximately 20-50 tokens per skill. The full SKILL.md body is loaded only when the skill is invoked. The `disable-model-invocation: true` flag prevents even the description from appearing in context. Subagents with preloaded skills are the exception: they receive full skill content at startup.
- **Quote**: "In a regular session, skill descriptions are loaded into context so Claude knows what's available, but full skill content only loads when invoked." — `code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md`
- **Quote**: From the GitHub issue where user skills were loading fully: "This was just a display/visual bug in `/context` command. Skills' token usage was incorrect. The bug was fixed in v2.1.1" — @PaulRBerg comment on issue #16616
- **Provenance**: Official docs + GitHub issue #16616

#### 9. The `skill({ name: '...' })` Call Pattern in SKILL.md

- **Confidence**: Medium — no source code found confirming this exact syntax; official docs do not mention it
- **Claim**: This pattern does not appear in the official skills documentation or in the leaked source for `SkillTool.ts`. The official invocation mechanism is either the user typing `/skill-name` or Claude calling the `Skill` tool with `{ skill: "name", args: "..." }`. Any `skill({ name: '...' })` pattern in a SKILL.md file is likely instructional text that Claude reads as a suggestion to invoke the Skill tool, not a real function call.
- **Provenance**: Could not find this syntax in any authoritative source

---

### Source Chain

1. `https://github.com/alex000kim/claude-code/blob/main/src/tools/SkillTool/SkillTool.ts` — Source code (leaked, March 31 2026 from npm v2.1.88 via Bun source map bug)
2. `https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md` — Official documentation
3. `https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/24858` — Maintainer-labeled bug report (closed), confirms two-path invocation model
4. `https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/42250` — Open bug report (April 2026), reproducible PreToolUse vs user-slash gap
5. `https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/16616` — Maintainer-labeled bug report (closed), confirms lazy-loading design was always the intent
6. `https://blakecrosley.com/blog/claude-code-source-leak` — Community analysis (practitioner), April 2026
7. `https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/` — Tech journalism with named security researcher analysis, confirms `SkillTool.ts:108` file path

---

### Conflicts

- Issue #16616 reporters claimed user skills were being fully loaded into context (high token counts in `/context`). @PaulRBerg (with references) said it was a `/context` display bug fixed in v2.1.1. The official docs assert lazy loading is the correct behavior. The source code for the skill listing mechanism is consistent with lazy loading. The display bug explanation is more authoritative since it was linked to a fix — but this means the display was misleading users about the actual behavior for a period.

- Issue #42250 shows PostToolUse fires on user slash commands while PreToolUse does not. Issue #24858 says neither fires. These reports are from different versions (v2.1.39 vs ~v2.2.x). The behavior may have partially changed between versions — PostToolUse may have been wired up for user-initiated paths while PreToolUse remains unwired.

---

### Gaps

- The `processPromptSlashCommand()` function body was not directly readable (the `processSlashCommand.js` GitHub URL returned 404 on the mirror). The exact mechanics of `!`command`` shell pre-execution (dynamic context injection) within that function are inferred from official docs, not confirmed via source.
- What happens to the `invocation_trigger` telemetry field for user-typed slash commands is unknown — the leaked source only shows `'claude-proactive'` being logged in `SkillTool.call()`, which does not execute for user-initiated paths.
- The `skill({ name: '...' })` syntax you asked about has no confirmed implementation — it may be something seen in community SKILL.md examples as a convention, not a real function. No source evidence found.
- How `context: fork` sub-agent results feed back into the parent conversation's token budget is not fully confirmed (the `mapToolResultToToolResultBlockParam` function in the forked path returns a `tool_result` block, which is the mechanism, but compaction behavior with forked skill results is inferred).

---

### Local Relevance

Your local skills are at `/Users/jeevanpillay/.claude/skills/` (confirmed in your git status: `.claude/skills/lightfast-changelog/`). Based on this analysis: those skills have their descriptions loaded into every session's context, and full SKILL.md bodies are only read when invoked. The `context: fork` and `allowed-tools` frontmatter on any skill you've authored will behave exactly as described above — `allowed-tools` mutates the live permission context for the session, not just Claude's judgment.

content.text:
I now have everything I need — the actual source code from the leak plus multiple GitHub issues confirming observed runtime behavior. This is comprehensive enough to give a definitive mechanical picture.

---

## Analysis: Claude Code Skill Execution Internals

### Summary

Skills in Claude Code are implemented as a real registered tool called `SkillTool` (internally `SKILL_TOOL_NAME`). When Claude auto-invokes a skill, it makes an actual tool call that Claude Code intercepts and resolves by injecting the rendered `SKILL.md` content as a `isMeta: true` user message into the conversation. When a user types `/skill-name` directly, the CLI bypasses the Skill tool entirely and injects the same user message via a pre-query processing path — which is why PreToolUse hooks do not fire on user-initiated slash commands but do fire on Claude-initiated ones. The `$ARGUMENTS` substitution is string interpolation performed before the content enters the conversation. The `context: fork` frontmatter spawns a genuine isolated sub-agent via `runAgent()`.

---

### Key Findings

#### 1. The Skill Tool is a Real Tool in Claude's Tool List

- **Confidence**: High — verified from leaked `SkillTool.ts` source code
- **Claim**: `SkillTool` is a proper tool object with input schema `{ skill: string, args?: string }`, registered in Claude's tool list alongside `BashTool`, `ReadTool`, etc. Claude can call it the same way it calls any other tool, with a `tool_use` block in its response.
- **Quote**: `export const SkillTool: Tool<InputSchema, Output, Progress> = buildTool({ name: SKILL_TOOL_NAME, searchHint: 'invoke a slash-command skill', ... })` — `src/tools/SkillTool/SkillTool.ts` (leaked source, alex000kim/claude-code mirror)
- **Provenance**: Leaked `SkillTool.ts` via Bun source map bug in npm package v2.1.88, March 31 2026

#### 2. Inline Skill Content Enters the Conversation as a Meta User Message

- **Confidence**: High — verified from leaked source
- **Claim**: For default (non-fork) skills, the `call()` method runs `processPromptSlashCommand()` which produces processed messages. These are tagged with `sourceToolUseID` and returned as `newMessages` on the `ToolResult`. They become `isMeta: true` user messages injected into the conversation history — not a system message, not a new assistant turn, but a specially-flagged user-role message. The official docs confirm this framing: "the rendered SKILL.md content enters the conversation as a single message and stays there for the rest of the session."
- **Quote**: For remote skills: `return { data: ..., newMessages: tagMessagesWithToolUseID([createUserMessage({ content: finalContent, isMeta: true })], toolUseID) }` — `SkillTool.ts` call() / `executeRemoteSkill()`
- **Provenance**: Leaked source + official docs at `code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md`

#### 3. $ARGUMENTS is Plain String Interpolation Before Injection

- **Confidence**: High — confirmed in source and official docs
- **Claim**: `$ARGUMENTS`, `$ARGUMENTS[N]`, `$N`, `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}`, and `${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}` are all string-substituted during `processPromptSlashCommand()` (or the equivalent rendering step) before the content is packaged as a message. The model never sees the placeholder tokens — it only receives the already-interpolated text. Shell-style quoting applies to indexed arguments.
- **Quote**: `finalContent = finalContent.replace(/\$\{CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR\}/g, normalizedDir); finalContent = finalContent.replace(/\$\{CLAUDE_SESSION_ID\}/g, getSessionId())` — `SkillTool.ts`, `executeRemoteSkill()` function
- **Provenance**: Leaked source + official docs string substitution table

#### 4. User-Typed `/skill-name` and Claude-Called `Skill(skill="name")` Are Two Different Code Paths

- **Confidence**: High — proven by GitHub bug reports with reproducible test cases
- **Claim**: When a user types `/skill-name` directly, the CLI processes it in the pre-query path (via `isSlashCommand()` detection in `messageQueueManager.js` and `processSlashCommand` before the API call), injecting the skill content as a user message before the query loop starts. The Skill tool is NOT invoked. When Claude auto-invokes, it issues a real `tool_use` block, which Claude Code handles via `SkillTool.call()`. This is why `PreToolUse: Skill` hooks fire on Claude-initiated calls but NOT on user-initiated `/skill-name` inputs. `UserPromptSubmit` is the only hook that fires for user-typed slash commands.
- **Quote**: "When a user types a slash command (e.g. `/commit`) directly, the Skill tool invocation does not trigger `PreToolUse` or `PostToolUse` hooks... When Claude programmatically invokes the same skill via the Skill tool, `PreToolUse:Skill` and `PostToolUse:Skill` fire as expected." — GitHub issue #24858 (confirmed reproduced)
- **Provenance**: Issues #24858, #42250 (open as of 2026-04-09), cross-referenced with `query.ts` source showing `isSlashCommand()` branch

#### 5. Claude Gets a Fresh Turn After Skill Content Is Injected

- **Confidence**: High — confirmed by official docs and source architecture
- **Claim**: The skill content arrives as a new user message in the conversation. Claude Code then makes a new API call with that expanded message history, giving Claude a fresh response turn to act on the instructions. It does not continue the same turn. The official docs state content "stays there for the rest of the session" — it is permanently part of the conversation history from that point forward, not a one-shot injection.
- **Quote**: "When you or Claude invoke a skill, the rendered SKILL.md content enters the conversation as a single message and stays there for the rest of the session. Claude Code does not re-read the skill file on later turns." — `code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md`
- **Provenance**: Official docs + consistent with tool result → new API call pattern visible in `query.ts`

#### 6. `context: fork` Spawns a Real Isolated Sub-Agent via `runAgent()`

- **Confidence**: High — verified from leaked source
- **Claim**: When a skill has `context: fork`, `SkillTool.call()` routes to `executeForkedSkill()`, which calls `prepareForkedCommandContext()` and then `runAgent()`. The sub-agent has its own isolated message history — it does NOT have access to the parent conversation. The skill content becomes the sub-agent's prompt. When the sub-agent completes, `extractResultText()` extracts a summary, which is returned as the Skill tool result to the parent conversation. This result is a `tool_result` block, not a user message.
- **Quote**: `const { modifiedGetAppState, baseAgent, promptMessages, skillContent } = await prepareForkedCommandContext(command, args || '', context); ... for await (const message of runAgent({ agentDefinition, promptMessages, ... })) { agentMessages.push(message) }` — `SkillTool.ts`, `executeForkedSkill()` function
- **Quote**: The `mapToolResultToToolResultBlockParam()` for forked skills returns `content: 'Skill "${result.commandName}" completed (forked execution).\n\nResult:\n${result.result}'` — same file
- **Provenance**: Leaked `SkillTool.ts`

#### 7. `allowed-tools` Grants Real Runtime Permissions, Not Just Hints

- **Confidence**: High — verified from source
- **Claim**: The `allowed-tools` frontmatter values are extracted from the processed command and stored in `allowedTools`. The `contextModifier` function in `SkillTool.call()` mutates `toolPermissionContext.alwaysAllowRules.command` to add those tools. This is a real permission grant active for the rest of the session after skill invocation, not advisory text. It does not restrict — all tools remain callable, and deny rules in settings still apply.
- **Quote**: `modifiedContext = { ...modifiedContext, getAppState() { const appState = previousGetAppState(); return { ...appState, toolPermissionContext: { ...appState.toolPermissionContext, alwaysAllowRules: { ...appState.toolPermissionContext.alwaysAllowRules, command: [...new Set([...existing, ...allowedTools])] } } } } }` — `SkillTool.ts`, `call()` contextModifier
- **Provenance**: Leaked source + official docs: "grants permission for the listed tools while the skill is active, so Claude can use them without prompting you for approval. It does not restrict which tools are available."

#### 8. Discovery vs. Full Load: Lazy Loading Is the Architecture

- **Confidence**: High — confirmed in official docs and GitHub issues
- **Claim**: At startup, only skill descriptions (frontmatter: name + description + when_to_use) are loaded into context as a skill listing — approximately 20-50 tokens per skill. The full SKILL.md body is loaded only when the skill is invoked. The `disable-model-invocation: true` flag prevents even the description from appearing in context. Subagents with preloaded skills are the exception: they receive full skill content at startup.
- **Quote**: "In a regular session, skill descriptions are loaded into context so Claude knows what's available, but full skill content only loads when invoked." — `code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md`
- **Quote**: From the GitHub issue where user skills were loading fully: "This was just a display/visual bug in `/context` command. Skills' token usage was incorrect. The bug was fixed in v2.1.1" — @PaulRBerg comment on issue #16616
- **Provenance**: Official docs + GitHub issue #16616

#### 9. The `skill({ name: '...' })` Call Pattern in SKILL.md

- **Confidence**: Medium — no source code found confirming this exact syntax; official docs do not mention it
- **Claim**: This pattern does not appear in the official skills documentation or in the leaked source for `SkillTool.ts`. The official invocation mechanism is either the user typing `/skill-name` or Claude calling the `Skill` tool with `{ skill: "name", args: "..." }`. Any `skill({ name: '...' })` pattern in a SKILL.md file is likely instructional text that Claude reads as a suggestion to invoke the Skill tool, not a real function call.
- **Provenance**: Could not find this syntax in any authoritative source

---

### Source Chain

1. `https://github.com/alex000kim/claude-code/blob/main/src/tools/SkillTool/SkillTool.ts` — Source code (leaked, March 31 2026 from npm v2.1.88 via Bun source map bug)
2. `https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md` — Official documentation
3. `https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/24858` — Maintainer-labeled bug report (closed), confirms two-path invocation model
4. `https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/42250` — Open bug report (April 2026), reproducible PreToolUse vs user-slash gap
5. `https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/16616` — Maintainer-labeled bug report (closed), confirms lazy-loading design was always the intent
6. `https://blakecrosley.com/blog/claude-code-source-leak` — Community analysis (practitioner), April 2026
7. `https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/` — Tech journalism with named security researcher analysis, confirms `SkillTool.ts:108` file path

---

### Conflicts

- Issue #16616 reporters claimed user skills were being fully loaded into context (high token counts in `/context`). @PaulRBerg (with references) said it was a `/context` display bug fixed in v2.1.1. The official docs assert lazy loading is the correct behavior. The source code for the skill listing mechanism is consistent with lazy loading. The display bug explanation is more authoritative since it was linked to a fix — but this means the display was misleading users about the actual behavior for a period.

- Issue #42250 shows PostToolUse fires on user slash commands while PreToolUse does not. Issue #24858 says neither fires. These reports are from different versions (v2.1.39 vs ~v2.2.x). The behavior may have partially changed between versions — PostToolUse may have been wired up for user-initiated paths while PreToolUse remains unwired.

---

### Gaps

- The `processPromptSlashCommand()` function body was not directly readable (the `processSlashCommand.js` GitHub URL returned 404 on the mirror). The exact mechanics of `!`command`` shell pre-execution (dynamic context injection) within that function are inferred from official docs, not confirmed via source.
- What happens to the `invocation_trigger` telemetry field for user-typed slash commands is unknown — the leaked source only shows `'claude-proactive'` being logged in `SkillTool.call()`, which does not execute for user-initiated paths.
- The `skill({ name: '...' })` syntax you asked about has no confirmed implementation — it may be something seen in community SKILL.md examples as a convention, not a real function. No source evidence found.
- How `context: fork` sub-agent results feed back into the parent conversation's token budget is not fully confirmed (the `mapToolResultToToolResultBlockParam` function in the forked path returns a `tool_result` block, which is the mechanism, but compaction behavior with forked skill results is inferred).

---

### Local Relevance

Your local skills are at `/Users/jeevanpillay/.claude/skills/` (confirmed in your git status: `.claude/skills/lightfast-changelog/`). Based on this analysis: those skills have their descriptions loaded into every session's context, and full SKILL.md bodies are only read when invoked. The `context: fork` and `allowed-tools` frontmatter on any skill you've authored will behave exactly as described above — `allowed-tools` mutates the live permission context for the session, not just Claude's judgment.

content.text:
Here's how Claude Code actually executes skills — it's simpler than you'd think:

---

## Claude Code Skill Execution (The Real Mechanics)

### The `Skill` Tool
Skills are a **real tool** in Claude's tool list (like Bash, Read, Edit). When Claude decides to invoke a skill, it makes an actual `tool_use` call: `Skill({ skill: "name", args: "..." })`.

### What happens on invocation:

1. **$ARGUMENTS** gets string-interpolated (plain find-and-replace)
2. The rendered SKILL.md content is injected as a **user message** (tagged `isMeta: true`) into the conversation
3. Claude gets a **fresh turn** to respond — it's not mid-turn continuation, it's a new API call with the expanded history
4. The skill content **stays permanently** in the conversation from that point forward

### Two paths for invocation:
- **User types `/skill-name`**: Pre-processed before the API call, content injected as user message. Skill tool is NOT called.
- **Claude auto-invokes**: Real `tool_use` block → Claude Code intercepts → injects content → new turn.

### `context: fork` (isolated execution):
Spawns a real sub-agent via `runAgent()` with its own message history. Parent conversation gets back a summary as a tool result. The sub-agent has no access to parent context.

### `allowed-tools`:
Actually mutates runtime permissions — not advisory. Grants tool auto-approval for the session.

### The `skill({ name: '...' })` pattern in your command files:
**Not a real function.** It's instructional text that Claude reads and interprets as "call the Skill tool with this name." Claude then makes the actual tool call.

---

## What This Means For Your Agent

Your agent runtime is **not interactive** — it's event-driven, single-pass. So you don't need the two-path complexity. The analog is:

1. Event arrives → build context (system prompt + SPEC.md + skill descriptions)
2. Agent decides: "this event warrants skill X"
3. Load full SKILL.md + command/*.md for skill X
4. **Inject as additional context** and re-run the agent with tools available
5. Agent executes (sends Slack message, writes memory, etc.)

It's basically the same as Claude Code's `context: fork` path — a fresh agent turn with skill content as the prompt, tools available, and the event as input. The key difference: no conversation history to persist (stateless per-event), but memory files bridge context across events.

Does this clarify the execution model you want to build? The main question is whether step 2 and step 4 are one LLM call or two (triage call → execution call).

content.content: Launching skill: research_codebase

content.text:
# Research Codebase

You are tasked with conducting comprehensive research across the codebase to answer user questions by spawning parallel sub-agents and synthesizing their findings.

## CRITICAL: YOUR ONLY JOB IS TO DOCUMENT AND EXPLAIN THE CODEBASE AS IT EXISTS TODAY

- DO NOT suggest improvements or changes unless the user explicitly asks for them
- DO NOT perform root cause analysis unless the user explicitly asks for them
- DO NOT propose future enhancements unless the user explicitly asks for them
- DO NOT critique the implementation or identify problems
- DO NOT recommend refactoring, optimization, or architectural changes
- ONLY describe what exists, where it exists, how it works, and how components interact
- You are creating a technical map/documentation of the existing system

## Initial Setup:

When this command is invoked, respond with:

```
I'm ready to research the codebase. Please provide your research question or area of interest, and I'll analyze it thoroughly by exploring relevant components and connections.
```

Then wait for the user's research query.

## Steps to follow after receiving the research query:

1. **Read any directly mentioned files first:**
   - If the user mentions specific files (tickets, docs, JSON), read them FULLY first
   - **IMPORTANT**: Use the Read tool WITHOUT limit/offset parameters to read entire files
   - **CRITICAL**: Read these files yourself in the main context before spawning any sub-tasks
   - This ensures you have full context before decomposing the research

2. **Analyze and decompose the research question:**
   - Break down the user's query into composable research areas
   - Take time to ultrathink about the underlying patterns, connections, and architectural implications the user might be seeking
   - Identify specific components, patterns, or concepts to investigate
   - Create a research plan using TodoWrite to track all subtasks
   - Consider which directories, files, or architectural patterns are relevant

3. **Spawn parallel sub-agent tasks for comprehensive research:**
   - Create multiple Task agents to research different aspects concurrently
   - We now have specialized agents that know how to do specific research tasks:

   **For codebase research:**
   - Use the **codebase-locator** agent to find WHERE files and components live
   - Use the **codebase-analyzer** agent to understand HOW specific code works (without critiquing it)
   - Use the **codebase-pattern-finder** agent to find examples of existing patterns (without evaluating them)

   **IMPORTANT**: All agents are documentarians, not critics. They will describe what exists without suggesting improvements or identifying issues.

   **For thoughts directory:**
   - Use the **thoughts-locator** agent to discover what documents exist about the topic
   - Use the **thoughts-analyzer** agent to extract key insights from specific documents (only the most relevant ones)

   **For web research (only if user explicitly asks):**
   - Use the **web-search-researcher** agent for external documentation and resources
   - IF you use web-research agents, instruct them to return LINKS with their findings, and please INCLUDE those links in your final report

   The key is to use these agents intelligently:
   - Start with locator agents to find what exists
   - Then use analyzer agents on the most promising findings to document how they work
   - Run multiple agents in parallel when they're searching for different things
   - Each agent knows its job - just tell it what you're looking for
   - Don't write detailed prompts about HOW to search - the agents already know
   - Remind agents they are documenting, not evaluating or improving

4. **Wait for all sub-agents to complete and synthesize findings:**
   - IMPORTANT: Wait for ALL sub-agent tasks to complete before proceeding
   - Compile all sub-agent results (both codebase and thoughts findings)
   - Prioritize live codebase findings as primary source of truth
   - Use thoughts/ findings as supplementary historical context
   - Connect findings across different components
   - Include specific file paths and line numbers for reference
   - Highlight patterns, connections, and architectural decisions
   - Answer the user's specific questions with concrete evidence

5. **Gather metadata for the research document:**
   - Get the current git commit hash: `git rev-parse HEAD`
   - Get the current branch: `git branch --show-current`
   - Get today's date
   - Filename: `thoughts/shared/research/YYYY-MM-DD-description.md`
     - Format: `YYYY-MM-DD-description.md` where:
       - YYYY-MM-DD is today's date
       - description is a brief kebab-case description of the research topic

6. **Generate research document:**
   - Use the metadata gathered in step 5
   - Structure the document with YAML frontmatter followed by content:

     ```markdown
     ---
     date: [Current date and time with timezone in ISO format]
     researcher: claude
     git_commit: [Current commit hash]
     branch: [Current branch name]
     topic: "[User's Question/Topic]"
     tags: [research, codebase, relevant-component-names]
     status: complete
     last_updated: [Current date in YYYY-MM-DD format]
     ---

     # Research: [User's Question/Topic]

     **Date**: [Current date and time with timezone]
     **Git Commit**: [Current commit hash]
     **Branch**: [Current branch name]

     ## Research Question

     [Original user query]

     ## Summary

     [High-level documentation of what was found, answering the user's question by describing what exists]

     ## Detailed Findings

     ### [Component/Area 1]

     - Description of what exists (`file.ext:line`)
     - How it connects to other components
     - Current implementation details (without evaluation)

     ### [Component/Area 2]

     ...

     ## Code References

     - `path/to/file.ts:123` - Description of what's there
     - `another/file.ts:45-67` - Description of the code block

     ## Architecture Documentation

     [Current patterns, conventions, and design implementations found in the codebase]

     ## Historical Context (from thoughts/)

     [Relevant insights from thoughts/ directory with references]

     - `thoughts/shared/something.md` - Historical decision about X

     ## Related Research

     [Links to other research documents in thoughts/shared/research/]

     ## Open Questions

     [Any areas that need further investigation]
     ```

7. **Add GitHub permalinks (if applicable):**
   - Check if on main branch or if commit is pushed: `git branch --show-current` and `git status`
   - If on main/master or pushed, generate GitHub permalinks:
     - Get repo info: `gh repo view --json owner,name`
     - Create permalinks: `https://github.com/{owner}/{repo}/blob/{commit}/{file}#L{line}`
   - Replace local file references with permalinks in the document

8. **Evaluate open questions (automatic):**
   - If the research document has an Open Questions section with items, this step runs automatically
   - Spawn the **open-questions-evaluator** agent with:
     - The path to the research document you just wrote
     - The path to `SPEC.md`
   - The evaluator will triage each question as **"now"**, **"defer"**, or **"already answered"**

   **For "now" questions:**
   - Use `AskUserQuestion` to surface the quick-answerable questions to the user:

     ```
     The research produced these open questions that can be answered quickly:
     1. [Question] — [evaluator's reason]
     2. [Question] — [evaluator's reason]

     Which would you like me to answer now? (all / numbers / none)
     ```

   - For each question the user approves, run `/research_codebase_lightweight` with:
     - The specific question
     - The parent research document path
     - The SPEC.md path
   - Run approved questions in parallel when possible
   - Each produces a separate followup research file linked from the parent

   **For "defer" questions:**
   - Leave them in the Open Questions section as-is
   - Append a note: `(deferred — needs dedicated research session)`

   **For "already answered" questions:**
   - Remove them from Open Questions and add the reference the evaluator found

   **If no open questions exist**, skip this step entirely.

9. **Present findings:**
   - Present a concise summary of findings to the user
   - Include key file references for easy navigation
   - Note any followup research files that were generated
   - Ask if they have follow-up questions or need clarification

10. **Handle follow-up questions:**
    - If the user has follow-up questions, append to the same research document
    - Update the frontmatter field `last_updated` to reflect the update
    - Add `last_updated_note: "Added follow-up research for [brief description]"` to frontmatter
    - Add a new section: `## Follow-up Research [timestamp]`
    - Spawn new sub-agents as needed for additional investigation
    - Continue updating the document

## Important notes:

- Always use parallel Task agents to maximize efficiency and minimize context usage
- Always run fresh codebase research - never rely solely on existing research documents
- The thoughts/ directory provides historical context to supplement live findings
- Focus on finding concrete file paths and line numbers for developer reference
- Research documents should be self-contained with all necessary context
- Each sub-agent prompt should be specific and focused on read-only documentation operations
- Document cross-component connections and how systems interact
- Include temporal context (when the research was conducted)
- Link to GitHub when possible for permanent references
- Keep the main agent focused on synthesis, not deep file reading
- Have sub-agents document examples and usage patterns as they exist
- Explore all of thoughts/ directory, not just research subdirectory
- **CRITICAL**: You and all sub-agents are documentarians, not evaluators
- **REMEMBER**: Document what IS, not what SHOULD BE
- **NO RECOMMENDATIONS**: Only describe the current state of the codebase
- **File reading**: Always read mentioned files FULLY (no limit/offset) before spawning sub-tasks
- **Critical ordering**: Follow the numbered steps exactly
  - ALWAYS read mentioned files first before spawning sub-tasks (step 1)
  - ALWAYS wait for all sub-agents to complete before synthesizing (step 4)
  - ALWAYS gather metadata before writing the document (step 5 before step 6)
  - NEVER write the research document with placeholder values
- **Frontmatter consistency**:
  - Always include frontmatter at the beginning of research documents
  - Keep frontmatter fields consistent across all research documents
  - Update frontmatter when adding follow-up research
  - Use snake_case for multi-word field names (e.g., `last_updated`, `git_commit`)
  - Tags should be relevant to the research topic and components studied


ARGUMENTS: Design the most accretive first step to implement an event-driven agent runtime that: 1) Triggers on platform/event.stored via Inngest, 2) Loads .lightfast/ config from the customer's GitHub repo (SPEC.md + skills/ + memory/), 3) Runs a single LLM triage call to decide what to do, 4) Can invoke a skill (load full SKILL.md + command/*.md, re-run agent with tools). The agent needs to touch: @repo/app-config (schema evolution), api/platform Inngest functions, the GitHub provider (tree reading + file writing), core/ai-sdk (agent creation pattern), and a new tool or two (sendNotification, writeMemory). Focus on what already exists and the minimal delta to get a vertical slice working end-to-end.
I'll conduct deep research on the existing codebase across every surface this will touch, then design the accretive first step.
